introduction: Cancer is abnormal and rapid cell growth with the potential to invade near or distant organs. It is the 2nd leading cause of death globally. Common adult cancers occur in the lung, breast, colon and rectum, liver, cervix, prostate and the ovaries. Some childhood cancers are leukaemias, lymphomas, Wilm's tumour, neuroblastoma, rhabdomyosarcoma and retinoblastoma. This section focuses mainly on adult tumours.

features: infiltration of surrounding tissues or structures
evidence of wider metastasis or dissemination

investigations: Tissue diagnosis is the hallmark of diagnosis, and it must be confirmed prior to initiation of treatment
Cytology: From body fluid or discharge or cell scrapings/shavings; fine needle aspiration biopsy/cytology (FNAB/C) of solid tumours
Histopathology: Core tissue obtained from specialized needles (e.g. Tru cut needle for solid tumours, Vim Silverman needle, Menghini needle), incisional/wedge biopsy, excision biopsy
Immunohistochemistry: Specialized studies to further characterize cancers e.g. breast cancer

non_drug: Involve palliative care team social workers, family support teams where available and as early as indicated
Comprehensive end-of-life care addressing physical, psychosocial, spiritual and existential needs and concerns of patients
Delivered at home or in hospital, ambulatory clinic, hospice or palliative care unit
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Adverse reactions due to treatment can lead to complications; referral to specialist care is advised
supportive_measures: Functional status and quality of life should be weighed against the benefit of chemotherapy

complications: Malignant pleural effusion and/or widespread lung parenchyma metastasis
Long bone metastasis and pathological fracture
Limb lymphoedema associated with breast cancer
prevention: Avoid tobacco smoking, chewing, snuffing, or passive smoking
Control of infections e.g. vaccination (e.g. HBV, HPV)
other_investigations: Screening: To identify individuals at risk of specific cancers when they are yet to occur or manifest. Generally, yearly for every one above 40 years of age
Breast: mammogram, breast ultrasound, clinical breast examination, breast self-examination, genetic testing e.g. BRCA
Colo-rectal: faecal occult blood test, colonoscopy, barium enema, digital rectal examination (DRE)
prognosis: Depends on tissue type, grade and immunohistochemistry
